Too many signs for me to ignore.

Morpho is becoming the default DeFi lending rail for institutions.

Coinbase, Kraken, and Bitwise are all using it for their products:

→ @coinbase powers its crypto-backed loans on it
→ @krakenfx's "DeFi Earn" runs on Morpho
→ @Bitwise curates lending vaults on it directly

The reason why is quite simple. Once a market goes live on @Morpho, the rules are immutable.

@aave is built around shared lending markets.
In comparison, Morpho is more modular: each market can have its own collateral, risk parameters, and curator.

That's why institutions are drawn to it. They can launch markets on Morpho on their exact terms.

Great perk to have in a TradFi cycle.

tweets.text:
On this day in 1215, King John sealed the Magna Carta at Runnymede. Most people remember it as a document about liberty and justice. It was really about money. Over 50 of its 63 clauses addressed specific financial grievances: illegal taxes, seized property, rigged courts that enriched the crown. The barons who forced John's hand weren't philosophers. They were landlords tired of getting shaken down.

The document established one principle that would outlast everything else in it: the king could not levy taxes without consent of the governed. That idea shaped the English Parliament, the American Revolution, and every tax debate since. When colonists dumped tea into Boston Harbor 558 years later, they were making the same argument the barons made at Runnymede.

It reads today like a founding text of property rights. At the time, it was a debt negotiation backed by the threat of civil war.

C’est une reconnaissance exceptionnelle, mais au-delà du classement, c’est surtout la consécration d’une aventure collective, portée par des femmes et des hommes profondément engagés : les étudiants, les alumni, les professeurs, les équipes pédagogiques, les équipes carrières et l’ensemble des partenaires qui font vivre ce programme au quotidien.

Ce résultat dit beaucoup de choses. Il dit la qualité académique du Master. Il dit la force d’un réseau international unique. Il dit l’exigence, l’ambition et l’énergie des étudiants. Il dit aussi la capacité de l’ESCP à former des profils capables de réussir dans un univers financier toujours plus compétitif, plus international et plus exigeant.

Être #1 mondial est déjà remarquable. Le rester quatre années de suite est le signe d’une vraie profondeur, d’une constance, et d’une culture de l’excellence qui ne se décrète pas mais qui se construit année après année.

Je suis particulièrement fier de cette réussite, fier d'avoir contribué à cette dynamique, et fier de voir l’ESCP continuer à rayonner au plus haut niveau mondial.

Un immense bravo à Philippe Thomas, à toutes les équipes du Master in Finance, aux étudiants, aux alumni et à tous ceux qui rendent cette réussite possible.

C’est une très belle page pour l’ESCP, et surtout une magnifique reconnaissance pour toute une communauté. 💙

*Lien (FT) : https://t.co/mEkfHu5idD

#ESCP #MasterInFinance #FinancialTimes #Finance

🇨🇳 #Housing | #China Cities Step Up Property Aid to Cut Buying Costs ⚠

➡ Chinese municipalities are intensifying property support measures by expanding housing subsidy programs and optimizing provident fund rules, moves aimed at lowering home-buying costs and stabilizing a sluggish real estate market, according to a report by the Securities Daily.

➡ Local governments across China have issued more than 470 property-related optimization policies so far this year, according to data from China Index Academy. 

➡ Of those, over 250 measures specifically targeted housing provident funds, focusing on raising loan ceilings, expanding withdrawal use cases, and supporting people with odd jobs or non-local workers.

➡ The continuous roll-out of local measures is successfully altering market sentiment despite short-term headwinds like summer heat and seasonal lulls. Yan Yuejin, vice president of the Shanghai E-House Real Estate Research Institute, told the paper that market expectations and confidence regarding price trends have “significantly strengthened,” lifting overall transaction activity to a new baseline.

➡ An official at the China Index Academy predicted that more cities will fast-track variations of these housing fund adjustments to help anchor the broader property market.

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/es8IbFFBVw

🇬🇧 In London, job openings for some white-collar roles have collapsed. Even if AI does not explain everything, the old model of knowledge work is clearly starting to break.

➡️ Some roles that used to sit at the heart of London’s service economy are now down to double-digit openings but we need to be careful as 2022 was a very specific period, with a still-tight labor market, post-Covid overhiring, tech euphoria and much more favorable financial conditions. Since then, rates have risen, the UK economy has slowed, banks and consulting firms have been looking to cut costs, and M&A/advisory activity has normalized.

🤔 In my view, AI is compressing the pyramid. Demand for senior profiles remains strong but junior generalists and repetitive task-based roles are much more exposed. The problem is that these tasks were also training tasks, so if companies cut junior cohorts too aggressively, they may improve margins in the short term but weaken their talent pipeline in the medium term.

The people who will come out ahead will not simply be those who use AI but those who know how to use it to become better at a real profession.

*Bloomberg link: https://t.co/VQU4kKAbsL

🇯🇵 The market is massively rebuilding one of the most powerful, but also one of the riskiest trades out there: shorting the yen 🚨

📉 Many investors are once again betting on further yen weakness, even as the currency trades near a very sensitive level around 160 against the dollar, and have built their largest bearish positions on the yen since 2017

➡️ Their logic is that as long as Japanese rates remain far below US rates, the yen carry trade remains attractive. Investors borrow in yen at a low cost, then reinvest that money into higher-yielding assets or currencies. As long as volatility stays low, this trade keeps paying, which is why the yen remains under pressure despite the Bank of Japan’s gradual rate hikes and despite the risk of FX intervention from Japanese authorities.

⚠️ Market now seems to see these risks as largely priced in. A BoJ rate hike is no longer a real surprise, neither is intervention from the Ministry of Finance. Many investors even see yen rebounds triggered by intervention as opportunities to sell the currency again. However, the more extreme the short yen positioning becomes, the more fragile the trade gets. If the BoJ turns more aggressive than expected, if US yields fall sharply, or if a risk-off episode forces funds to reduce leverage, the yen can rebound violently because too many investors are positioned in the same direction.

The yen can still weaken gradually from here but the risk of a brutal squeeze rises as bearish positioning keeps building.

*Bloomberg link: https://t.co/m7tuV1Fb0C

tweets.text:
Today, there's a new report that China eased InP substrate exports. 

Which is expected to relieve mass production bottlenecks in the photonics market (source: Digitimes)

My optical positions are very happy to hear this:

From $AXTI (substrates), $IQE (epiwafers) to $AAOI (lasers) / $LITE / $SIVE, and others. 

Taiwanese optical players like VPEC, Landmark, and others should go brrr as well. 

Just to recap: the photonics market, especially for laser companies is moreso "how much can you make" rather than how much demand is there. 

InP substrates was one of the main bottlenecks affecting upstream capacity.   

So if you're able to make more = more revenue.

Just to recap: the photonics market, especially for laser companies is moreso "how much can you make" rather than how much demand is there. 

InP substrates was one of the main bottlenecks affecting upstream capacity.   

So if you're able to make more = more revenue.

Not a fan of poet, but even if their tech doesn't get qualified into hyperscalers, they can always acquire other companies to scale.

tweets.text:
I'll be the first to admit that trying to predict anything based on Trump's decisions is a fool's game. The man is more volatile than the shitcoins he launched himself.

But if the Iran peace deal actually gets signed on Friday, I believe we are looking at the start of a massive bull rally.

Historically, almost every major war ending triggered a massive rally in risk assets:

Korean War: S&P +44% in the year after. 
Gulf War: massive relief rally. 
Iraq War: S&P +25% in year one. 

In 19 out of 20 post-WWII conflicts, markets fully recovered in an average of just 28 days.

The Iran war has been without a doubt the biggest macro headwind for markets, so it's not surprising appetite for risk is collapsing the most. Bitcoin is sitting at $65k, down 48% from its $126k all time high. Most altcoins are looking much much worse.

But looks like that headwind is about to get lifted, and crypto as the highest beta risk asset on the planet will be the first to reprice when risk appetite comes flooding back.

Everyone who's been looking like an idiot for the last few months will soon look like a genius.

tweets.text:
Macro for Dummies, by Dummies

Just six trading days ago, after the Nasdaq had already fallen roughly 8%, quite a few investment banks and well-known investors started raising caution flags on the market.

For instance, firms such as Bank of America and Citigroup, along with investors such as Steve Eisman of “The Big Short” fame, were warning about the risks ahead.

The concerns sounded convincing: no rate cuts, the $SPCX IPO valuation, capital raising concerns around $GOOGL, $META, and $ORCL, and a long list of other “red flags.”

Looking back, that turned out to be the bottom.

Including today’s pre-market action following the signing of a peace deal, the Nasdaq has rallied roughly 6% since then.

Now the response will be: “Well, nobody expected a peace deal.”

Exactly.

If the entire forecasting exercise is just extrapolating today’s conditions into the future, and the future almost never unfolds the way the present looks, what’s the point of doing it in the first place?

The only forecast with a perfect long-term track record is that markets tend to go up over time.

If someone asks what the market will do next week, next month, or after the next macro event, the honest answer is:

“I don’t know.”

The future is always shaped by variables nobody is talking about yet.

Ironically, the people on X who obsess over macro are usually the most clueless participants in the market.

They spend their days repeating assumptions, quoting each other, and recycling whatever happens to be popular on the timeline that week.

Very few have actually outperformed the market. Yet somehow they’re always the most certain about what happens next.

thread_tweets.text:
Market cycles are one of the vaguest ideas in investing. A cycle can last 5 years, 10 years, or 80 years. Nobody knows.

Every time the market goes up, someone says we’re in the middle of a cycle. Every time it goes down, someone says the cycle is ending.

Look at Ray Dalio. He’s been talking about cycles for years while the market kept making new all time highs.

In investing, “cycle” is often just another word for “I don’t know what’s going to happen next.”

This is fascinating.

TL;DR

> Networks of smaller AI models are outperforming frontier models 
> The cheapest way to get Fable performance is any permutation of GPT and Fable
> The playbook is to take any frontier AI model, find the next-best (cheaper) frontier AI model, ensemble it with the leading open source model, and now you’ve got a cheaper version of the frontier
>  So called “Frontier AI Companies” will never again achieve the accuracy/cost/speed frontier
> The frontier is now owned by the network of leading models and companies
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
This is a *way* bigger deal than it seems... 

Frontier AI companies will *never* own the frontier again

I kid you not... I've been waiting for someone to show this result for like 4 years... this is a huge deal.

The short reason: combinations of models will *always* outperform

the beautiful thing about this is satya’s AI vision is extremely pro-human

ai models don’t work without the humans guiding taste, setting goals, following instinct etc

the models are completely soulless without context and intents. thats why ai agents suck for most people right now. they’re lacking important instinct to WHY a human makes a decision in the workplace or at home. 

most people use ai as a google search replacement - the market to customise these models into a human co-worker is huge but difficult

the answer (in satyas vision) is the learning harness:

- combination of the ai model + context of work place + recursive learning loop

a self improving company.

tweets.text:
hang on this has got to be one of the most significant advances in AI recently - using a committee of (often dumber) models can achieve claude fable 5 performance for 50% of the cost but also beats fable?

its not because of the models themselves either, its the "judgement model" :

> openrouter's new fusion api basically sends your prompt to multiple models. a judgement model aggregates the answers then synthesizes the best answer

> the synthesis part is basically where the magic is. basically "two heads are better than one". 

> they made opus 4.8 talk to itself and that resulted in a 6 point boost in benchmark results.

its worth noting this wasn't achieved on long-horizon tasks (fable still smokes at this)

but for any company figuring out which model to use perhaps consider the answer might be *all of them*
